chapters.title: Treat criticism as fuel for improvement
chapters.text:
Some customers never praise good service but leap at any chance to vent when expectations fall short. Jay Baer calls them haters — people who deliver negative feedback about your products or operations. The easy move for support teams is to ignore the loudest complaints. That's like tossing all the salt because the dish might get too salty.

The more grievances a brand receives, the more intelligence it gathers to beat competitors who guess instead of listen.

Customer satisfaction should come before profit in how you allocate attention. Complaints are data, not trash to delete. Twenty-first-century markets are more crowded than ever; standout service is one of the few durable differentiators left when products look alike on a spreadsheet.

Put users first. Treat every critique as insight that sharpens your offer and reveals blind spots internal teams miss because they live inside the product every day.

This summary aims to make business owners welcome negative feedback instead of dreading it. Haters hurt to like — until you see complaints as catalysts for growth rather than attacks on your ego. Criticism helps because it:

• Brings outside perspective straight to your desk without a consulting invoice.
• Shows how the public actually sees you, not how slide decks describe you.
• Points to fixes that serve customers better and reduce repeat complaints.

Inside you'll find clear-cut strategies for reframing criticism and putting it to work across channels your team already monitors — or should be monitoring tomorrow.

For the majority of businesses, the only reason we can eke by with our current customer service approach is because our competitors are equally shabby, or worse. ~ Jay Baer

chapters.title: Great experiences keep customers coming back
chapters.text:
Treating every complaint as purely negative wastes its value and trains teams to hide from hard feedback. Ignoring the upside of grievances means losing retention, reputation, and revenue you already paid to acquire.

Every review — positive or harsh — reveals how outsiders perceive your company and gives you a chance to correct course before churn spreads through word of mouth.

A complaint handled well can turn a furious buyer into a loyal advocate who tells friends you came through when it mattered.

The cost of retaining customers is often higher than winning new ones, yet consumers vanish quickly when complaints are ignored or brushed off with copy-paste replies. Build a strong service layer by:

• Designing accessible support channels people actually want to use.
• Owning mistakes openly instead of lawyering the language.
• Listening until unhappy callers feel heard, not processed.
• Changing processes where patterns emerge across multiple tickets.

When clients feel genuinely attended to, you save marketing dollars and turn former critics into ambassadors. Most satisfied buyers will recommend a business they trust; disappointed ones warn their networks just as loudly. Investing in customer service is therefore both impression management and a retention strategy for the long haul.

Answering complaints increases customer advocacy across all customer service channels. ~ Jay Baer

Did you know? A 2020 Wall Street Journal survey found 43% of customers used email, live chat, or social media to file complaints.

chapters.title: Creative responses turn complaints into productivity
chapters.text:
Dissatisfied customers complain more readily than satisfied ones leave praise — an asymmetry every support leader should plan for. Baer sorts haters into two groups:

• Onstage haters air grievances publicly — social posts, review sites, open forums where the audience is the point.
• Offstage haters reach out privately through email, phone, or direct messages expecting a direct reply.

Most firms prefer offstage noise, but critics choose their own stage. You need a plan for both, not a hope that everyone emails politely.

In today's world, social media is a primary gauge of customer experience and brand health — a live scoreboard competitors and shoppers read before they buy.

Younger buyers vent on Facebook, Twitter, Yelp, and similar platforms where screenshots travel fast. Older customers more often email or call support lines they trust. A phone call or detailed email usually signals an expected reply — if someone calls about inflated call rates, they want an answer now, not next week.

A tagged rant like "ABC network is unreliable" may be venting without demanding dialogue — yet a fast, thoughtful response still surprises and wins goodwill from lurkers who never wrote a word.

Callers need explanations; social posters may only want attention. Whether it's a public or private complaint, a prompt reply that defies the expectation of customers would always leave them in awe.

Most companies would prefer receiving complaints offstage rather than in public spaces. However, the fact remains that people would do their critiquing via various platforms for various reasons, hence the need for businesses to embrace both.

chapters.title: Public replies should reflect your brand vision
chapters.text:
When consumers raise concerns about products or brands, businesses must be aware that these users are speaking up for others who would never speak. When customers complain in public, they often speak for silent observers too — people with the same question who will never open a ticket. Choosing Twitter over your helpline invites the world to judge between you and the buyer. Silence shapes how prospects see you, and rarely in your favor.

Answering openly can replace ad spend if done right because every thread is a billboard. Public responses buy you:

• A shot at keeping the complainer instead of losing them quietly.
• A chance to win onlookers who never spoke up but were wavering.

A rash response or total silence can scare away potential customers who assume you'll treat them the same way.

Many dissatisfied customers leave without saying a word. Sometimes they stay only because they read how you helped someone else with the same issue. So onstage replies must address the audience, not just the original poster.

Offering a discount or store credit after a service failure can restore faith and prevent churn when the fix matches the harm. For instance, a customer who complained about bad service may be given a discount or store credit for their next purchase, renewing their faith in the company and preventing churn.

When the expectations of most customers are not met, they walk away without even complaining. More often than not, the only way the patronage of this set of people is retained is when they come across a response given to another customer, which may be all the clarification they needed.

When you address onstage complaints (or even acknowledge positive or neutral feedback) you have a chance to dramatically boost customer advocacy. ~ Jay Baer

chapters.title: Remove the barriers to excellent service
chapters.text:
Few companies truly "hug their haters" — answering every complaint on every channel, every time. Common obstacles explain why:

• Too many channels

The internet multiplied feedback platforms and made cross-brand comparison effortless for shoppers. Firms that can't respond quickly everywhere lose ground to rivals who look more attentive. Meet customers on whichever channel they choose while staying competitive on speed.

Embracing customer complaints and interacting with them on every platform is the secret to creating a great customer experience.

• Too much feedback

Volume overwhelms many teams, especially on public channels where complaints compound visually. Ask customers to help one another through community forums or peer-support programs so agents focus on escalations.

• No customer experience culture

Another reason for non-responsiveness to customer interaction is the lack of hospitality culture. Acquisition often gets more budget than retention, yet keeping buyers takes extra effort — sometimes leaving your comfort zone to solve weird edge cases. JetBlue proved the payoff: a passenger tweeted before a flight about looking forward to Popchips, only to find the plane stocked out. She tweeted disappointment mid-flight over in-air Wi-Fi. JetBlue mailed a box of Popchips with a note to her home. Days later she posted a photo thanking them — free marketing from a snack rescue.

Much commitment is required to provide excellent customer service, but it is a great necessity if you want your company to grow.

The cost of acquiring customers most times is less than the cost of retaining them. Hence, many companies would rather focus on attracting clients than keeping them, as it would take extra effort to retain them. Several times, to meet the specific needs of a customer, the company must make the extra effort that would often take them out of their comfort zone. For instance, JetBlue airlines had a passenger who tweeted before her flight about looking forward to getting Popchips — one of the free snacks served on the flights.

chapters.title: Handle complaints professionally to keep trust
chapters.text:
Unhappy buyers speak up when service or product quality falls short. If stellar customer experience is your north star, resolving those concerns moves to the front of the queue — ahead of campaigns, roadmaps, and internal politics.

Build a response system that works in practice:

• Open clear contact paths — phone, email, web forms, chat — so grievances land where your team can see them.
• Staff with people who de-escalate well and protect both the customer relationship and brand reputation.
• Spot issues fast. Private complaints arrive in your inbox; public ones require deliberate monitoring of reviews and social mentions.
• Reply with calm professionalism. Acknowledge the problem, outline next steps, and keep tone respectful even when the customer is not.

Public complaints are harder to catch than inbox tickets. It is nearly impossible to track every comment manually, so prioritize platforms where your buyers congregate and invest in software that flags brand mentions and tags automatically.

Private complaints surface in the systems you built — helplines, support queues, contact forms. Onstage feedback demands active searching across review sites and social feeds where your product category draws commentary.

Your objective in any dispute: soothe the person, solve the underlying problem, and resist the urge to score debate points. Winning the argument while losing the customer is a hollow victory.

Never answer a public gripe only in private — you may salvage one relationship while losing everyone reading the thread. When sensitive data such as account numbers must be exchanged, post a brief public acknowledgment first, then invite the customer to a secure channel for the details.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
Unlike in the nineteenth and twentieth centuries, when customer complaints were few and strategic, they have now tripled or even quadrupled — thanks to multiple social platforms broadcasting every gripe.

Rivals use that visibility to outpace you, yet the flood is also an advantage if you treat it as free research.

Complainants should not be seen as people trying to discredit your brand just for the sake of having a valid strategy. Instead, see your haters as people observing and bringing feedback on how you can improve your products and stand out from your competitors. Keep your eyes on the positive outcomes you can create with that single feedback. With one negative (or positive) piece of feedback, you can:

• Learn. Use feedback to see where you are doing well and areas where you need to improve from a different perspective.
• Retain. Customers complain when they are dissatisfied. But you can use their complaints as an opportunity to retain or win them back.
• Market. Recognize that each complaint and response given on social media or review sites is not just limited to the company and the complainant but to the general public.

When customers bring in their complaints and feedback, they expect to meet with the actual personalities behind the brand. If this person replies to them shabbily or ignores them, this paints an ugly picture of the company. However, if the customer service attends to customers' complaints with respect and courtesy, this can retain the clients.

Try this

When a complaint arrives, respond within twenty-four hours — publicly if the complaint was public. Reply as soon as possible so customers know you're listening and already acting. Swing into motion immediately, then check back to ensure they were satisfied with the outcome.

Read it till the end?
